unitTitle: Y8 Media — vector graphics (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Get into shapes — drawing and ordering vector shapes
lessons.objectives:
I can name vector graphics and the software used to make them
I can use the shape tools to draw and change shapes
I can change a shape's fill and outline
I can move, rotate, and reorder shapes using z-order
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: vector · fill · stroke · rotate · z-order · handle · open source · cross-platform. TA: everyone has Inkscape open and a saved .svg. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — a picture made of shapes (the house image). Pupils TICK the shapes they see (multi-select, no writing).
   • Support: tick-box choice.  • Core: estimate shapes in a robot.  • Challenge: a picture from only circles + triangles.
2. (5–10) I-do — what is a vector graphic + the software (Inkscape logo). Plain meaning of open source / cross-platform.
3. (10–25) You-do — draw shapes with the three tools (rectangle, ellipse, star/polygon). Worksheet label-a-diagram names the shapes; the order block sequences “draw then colour a shape”.
   • Support: word-bank/tick.  • Core: name the shapes.  • Challenge: explorer task — what the handles do. Likely error: tool stays selected → fix-words “switch to the black arrow to select.” TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (25–40) Change fill and outline (Fill/Stroke tabs image). fill = inside colour, stroke = the edge line.
   • Support: choose fill vs stroke.  • Core: explain z-order/handles.  • Challenge: change the dash style.
5. (40–50) Move, rotate, and order — z-order (which shape is in front). Pupils save their .svg and screenshot it on the activity worksheet.
6. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Working with multiple objects — align, group, combine
lessons.objectives:
I can select more than one object at once
I can align and distribute objects to line them up
I can group and ungroup objects to work with them together
I can combine shapes using union, difference, and intersection
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: object · align · distribute · group · combine · union · difference · intersection. TA: Inkscape open, last lesson's skills recalled. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — stacks of shapes (a 3-stripe flag = rectangles). Pupils CHOOSE the count (no writing). Recap “object”.
2. (5–10) I-do — why work with many objects (saves time, neat, move parts together).
3. (10–20) You-do — align and distribute. Select-many first (rubber-band or Shift-click), then line up / space evenly.
   • Support: choose what align/group do.  • Core: explain distribute.  • Challenge: reason about z-order. Likely error: only one object selected → fix-words “drag a box around them all first.”
4. (20–30) Group and ungroup (grouped-objects image). A group can be ungrouped any time.
5. (30–45) Combine — union/difference/intersection (the crescent image). The worksheet card-sort (group vs combine) and the matching grid (which combine does what). Combining makes ONE object that cannot be ungrouped. Pupils screenshot a result.
6. (45–55) Plenary — hands-up: name what union/difference/intersection do; self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Same task, low floor/high ceiling; no flashing/sound; movement breaks routine. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.

lessons.title: Paths — nodes, lines, and editing
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that vector graphics are made of paths and nodes
I can draw and edit straight, curved, and freehand paths
I can convert a shape into a path and edit its nodes
I can use paths and shapes to make a superhero face
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: path · node · freehand · object · convert. TA: Inkscape open; exit-ticket slips ready for the plenary. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — combining shapes recap (the square+triangle set: union/difference/intersection results). Pupils CHOOSE which combine made each result (no writing).
2. (5–10) I-do — what is a path (a route between points; nodes mark the start, end, and each turn).
3. (10–25) You-do — draw straight, curved, and freehand paths; edit by dragging nodes. The worksheet order block sequences “draw a closed path”.
   • Support: name node/path/freehand.  • Core: straight vs curved; why convert first.  • Challenge: what the curve handles do. Likely error: path not closed → fix-words “click back on the first node.”
4. (25–35) Convert a shape to a path (Path ▷ Object to Path), then edit its nodes — make the head outline, send it to the back.
5. (35–50) Make a superhero face (the worked-example image) using paths + converted shapes. Pupils save and screenshot it.
6. (50–55) Plenary — exit ticket (one key word + meaning) collected for L4's starter; self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Same task, low floor/high ceiling; no flashing/sound. TA: prompt, do not draw it for them.

lessons.title: What will you make? — plan and create a vector project
lessons.objectives:
I can describe logos, icons, and illustrations
I can choose a project and plan a design
I can combine my tools and techniques to create a vector image
I can evaluate my project against its purpose
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: logo · icon · illustration · plan · purpose · rubric. TA: pairs decided; activity sheet + rubric ready. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — exit tickets from L3. Pupils MATCH key words to meanings (no long writing). Agree / disagree / add.
2. (5–10) I-do — logos, icons, illustrations (three example images). The worksheet card-sort groups examples into the three types.
3. (10–25) Plan — choose a project (zoo icons / theme-park logo / book illustration); sketch a design idea; read the rubric (what is the purpose?).
   • Support: choose what a logo/icon is.  • Core: state the purpose + one fitting feature.  • Challenge: why a vector suits “works large or small”; name two techniques to use.
4. (25–50) Create — paired making in Inkscape using Lessons 1–3 skills. Apply a few skills well. Pupils screenshot progress.
5. (50–55) Plenary — share with another pair (one easy, one tricky, one tip); confidence slider on the worksheet; self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Same task, low floor/high ceiling; no flashing/sound; movement breaks routine.

lessons.title: Behind the scenes — how a vector is stored (SVG markup)
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that a vector is stored as markup (text)
I can change an object by editing its markup
I can explain what SVG and “scalable” mean
I can plan and make improvements to my project
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: markup · scalable · SVG · path. TA: Inkscape open; projects from L4 ready. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — spot the difference: MATCH each rubric statement to icon/illustration/logo (no writing).
2. (5–20) I-do — inside an SVG. A vector is stored as readable text (markup). Show <rect x y width height /> (the shapes image). x,y = position; width,height = size. The worksheet fill-in-the-blank captures this.
   • Support: markup = text that describes the image; scalable = bigger/smaller without losing quality.  • Core: what SVG stands for; what x/y do.  • Challenge: edit markup to double a square (code answer); why vectors scale but photos blur.
3. (20–25) Scalable — double the numbers, double the square. Because it is numbers, it scales perfectly.
4. (25–30) Edit markup in Inkscape — change a value, the shape changes (draw a square, set 80×80, duplicate half-size and double-size).
5. (30–50) Review + develop the project against the rubric and last lesson's feedback; pupils screenshot progress.
6. (50–55) Plenary — two recall questions; self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Same task, low floor/high ceiling; no flashing/sound. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.

lessons.title: Showcase — vector vs bitmap, evaluate and share
lessons.objectives:
I can explain the key differences between vector and bitmap images
I can say which image type suits which use
I can evaluate my image against a rubric
I can share a finished project
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: vector · bitmap · pixel · scalable. TA: rubric + finished projects ready. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — good, or could be better? Judge the Treasure Island logo against the rubric (CHOOSE yes/no + one improvement).
2. (5–20) I-do — vectors vs bitmaps (clownfish vector vs photo; the pixels-zoom image). bitmap = pixels (fine detail, blurs when enlarged); vector = paths (stays sharp). Fonts are vectors.
   • Support: what each is made of.  • Core: one difference; why fonts scale.  • Challenge: characteristics + SVG quiz; pick the right type for a scenario and justify.
3. (20–25) Which type suits which use — the worksheet card-sort (vector-best vs bitmap-best). Some uses fit both.
4. (25–45) Improve + evaluate — small final changes, then self/peer-assess against the rubric. Pupils screenshot the finished project.
5. (45–50) Quick quiz — worksheet questions (or the source 14-question assessment for a formal mark). Confidence slider on the worksheet.
6. (50–55) Showcase — share projects; what works well, what could improve; self-tick the 4 “I can…”. Same task, low floor/high ceiling; no flashing/sound; movement breaks routine.
